title: Windows BitLockerToGo with Network Activity
spl:
`sysmon` EventCode=22 process_name="bitlockertogo.exe"
      | stats count min(_time) as firstTime max(_time) as lastTime
        BY answer answer_count dvc
           process_exec process_guid process_name
           query query_count reply_code_id
           signature signature_id src
           user_id vendor_product QueryName
           QueryResults QueryStatus
      | `security_content_ctime(firstTime)`
      | `security_content_ctime(lastTime)`
      | `windows_bitlockertogo_with_network_activity_filter`
description: The following analytic detects suspicious usage of BitLockerToGo.exe, which has been observed being abused by Lumma stealer malware. The malware leverages this legitimate Windows utility to manipulate registry keys, search for cryptocurrency wallets and credentials, and exfiltrate sensitive data. This activity is significant because BitLockerToGo.exe provides functionality for viewing, copying, and writing files as well as modifying registry branches - capabilities that the Lumma stealer exploit…

implementation: ESCU Hunting detection — not designed for automated alerting. Run ad-hoc or on a low-frequency schedule from Splunk Enterprise Security for analyst-driven investigation. Requires Sysmon EventID 22 ingested and CIM-normalized.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Resolution.DNS
  by DNS.src DNS.query span=1h
| sort -count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration on Network_Resolution (DNS) before using this tstats search.
knownFalsePositives: High DNS query volume from browsers, auto-updaters, and load tests; allowlist well-known resolvers and SaaS patterns.
detailedImplementation:
Enterprise Security Detection Rule

"Windows BitLockerToGo with Network Activity" is a Hunting detection designed for analyst-driven threat hunting rather than automated alerting, sourced from the Splunk Enterprise Security Content Update (ESCU). Hunting detections are not intended for automated alerting. They are run on-demand or on a low-frequency schedule by security analysts investigating specific threat hypotheses.

Prerequisites

• Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x or later with the ES Content Update (ESCU) app installed and up to date.
• Data sources: Sysmon EventID 22. Must be ingested into Splunk and normalized to the Common Information Model (CIM) via the appropriate Technology Add-on.
• MITRE ATT&CK: T1218. Review the ATT&CK matrix for adjacent techniques to identify detection coverage gaps in your environment.

Deployment

1. In Enterprise Security, navigate to Configure → Content → Content Management.
2. Search for "Windows BitLockerToGo with Network Activity" or filter by Analytic Story.
3. Hunting detections are typically left disabled for automated scheduling. Instead, run them on-demand from the Search bar or schedule at low frequency (daily or weekly).
4. Review results manually — hunting detections cast a wider net and expect analyst judgment to separate signal from noise.
5. When results warrant further investigation, create a Notable Event manually or initiate your incident response workflow.

Tuning and False Positive Management

Known false positives for this detection: High DNS query volume from browsers, auto-updaters, and load tests; allowlist well-known resolvers and SaaS patterns.

• Hunting detections are expected to produce broader result sets that require analyst interpretation. Focus on refining the search scope (time range, specific hosts or users) rather than eliminating all noise.
• Maintain a hunting journal documenting hypotheses tested, results found, and detection improvements identified.
• Share high-fidelity findings with the detection engineering team to convert recurring hunting patterns into automated TTP or Anomaly detections.

Analyst Response Workflow

When reviewing Hunting results:

1. Run the search manually or review scheduled results. Evaluate each returned event against your threat hypothesis — not every result indicates compromise.
2. Cross-reference findings with current threat intelligence, recent security advisories, and outputs from other detection sources.
3. If suspicious activity is confirmed, create a Notable Event or escalate directly to your incident response process with the supporting evidence.
4. Document the hunting exercise: hypothesis, data sources queried, time range, findings, and any detection engineering improvements identified.

Validation

After the search returns events, sample a few rows and compare the same host and time in your EDR console (CrowdStrike Falcon, Microsoft Defender for Endpoint portal, or Carbon Black) so usernames, process names, and dispositions line up. If Sysmon or Windows Security are listed in Data sources, open the Sysmon Operational log in Event Viewer and confirm the matching process, registry, or network line.
grandmaExplanation: We use endpoint security telemetry to stay ahead of the behavior this rule calls “Windows BitLockerToGo with Network Activity”, so we catch real tradecraft and not just noise from everyday work.
